scenario: The patient is a 55-year-old gentleman who presents for further evaluation of right leg weakness
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Hi there! I am Doctor Jones, sir."] Hello! It is nice to meet you.
[Clinician asked: "What brings you into see me today?"] I have had this weakness in my right leg for quite some time now.

[Clinician asked: "How long has this been going on and do you know how you injured yourself?"] I think that it was about six months ago that the weakness in my leg started. I don't really remember how it happened.

[Clinician asked: "Can you tell me what you do remember?"] I was reaching to get something from a cabinet, and I noticed that I was unable to stand on my right toe. Ever since then I have had difficulty pushing off when walking.  My toes were tingling and numb.
[Clinician asked: "Was the numbness and tingling mild, moderate, or severe?"] It was a mild feeling, but this has been an ongoing problem that has been the same since the weakness started.
[Clinician asked: "Have you had any other pain any where else in your body?"] I have had back pain, but this has been going on for many years and has not changed.
[Clinician asked: "Is the back pain been mild, moderate, or severe?"] I would say mild. I have also been having cramping in both calves.
[Clinician asked: "How long has that been going on?"] For the past year but it stopped about two months ago.
[Clinician asked: "Have you had any weakness in the left leg?"] No. I don't think so.

[Clinician asked: "Have you had any pain around right leg and the area of numbness and tingling?"] No. Everything that is going on with my right leg has pretty much been the same since it started six months ago. I have not gotten worse or better.
[Clinician asked: "Have you had any bowel or bladder incontinence? Any radicular pain?"] No.

[Clinician asked: "When did you first see a doctor for the issue? I remember that you told me you saw a different provider prior to coming to see me."] I saw the other doctor in October. That was a couple of months before I came to see you.

[Clinician asked: "I see in your chart that we did a CT scan that shows degenerative changes, but nothing obviously abnormal. Remind me why we did not do a MRI of your spine?"] I cannot have an MRI because of my ear implant.

[Clinician asked: "That is unfortunate. It looks like we were able to do an EMG and nerve conduction studies. What medications are you taking currently?"] I had an elevated CK before I started taking stat medications.
[Clinician asked: "What medications are those?"] I am not taking Lipitor anymore. I think I took it for about fifteen months.

[Clinician asked: "Do you know what your levels of your CK were before you started taking medication?"] I thought that my CK was in the five hundred or six hundreds prior to starting it. Once I started it, my levels increased to about eight hundred and then came down to about five hundred again when it was stopped. I recently stopped taking Lipitor because my levels were up to the thousand's and then my CK apparently has returned to about the five hundreds or six hundreds.
[Clinician asked: "I don't see any labs in your chart. Did your primary do labs for your CK levels?"] Yes, he did.
[Clinician asked: "Okay. Have you started any other medications?"] Yes, I just started Zetia.
[Clinician asked: "Have you experienced any weakness or pain after starting Zetia?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "Can you remind me how old you are?"] I am fifty-five.

gold_sections.HPI: The patient is a 55-year-old gentleman who presents for further evaluation of right leg weakness.  He has difficulty recollecting the exact details and chronology of his problem.  To the best of his recollection, he thinks that about six months ago he developed weakness of his right leg.  He describes that he is reaching to get something from a cabinet and he noticed that he was unable to stand on his right toe.  Since that time, he has had difficulty pushing off when he walks.  He has mild tingling and numbness in his toes, but this has been a chronic problem and nothing new since he has developed the weakness.  He has chronic mild back pain, but this has been persistent for many years and has not changed.  He has experienced cramps in both calves for the past year.  This dissipated about two months ago.  He does not think that his left leg is weak.  He does not have any bowel or bladder incontinence.  There is no radicular pain.  He does not think that the problem is progressive, meaning that the weakness that he perceives in his right leg is no different than when it was six months ago. He first sought medical attention for this problem in October.  He then saw you a couple of months later.  He has undergone an EMG and nerve conduction studies.  Unfortunately, he cannot undergo an MRI of his spine because he has an ear implant.  He has had a CT scan that shows degenerative changes, but nothing obviously abnormal. In addition, the patient has hyperCKemia.  He tells me that he has had an elevated CK prior to starting taking stat medications, although this is not entirely clear to me.  He thinks that he is not taking Lipitor for about 15 months and thought that his CK was in the 500 or 600s prior to starting it.  Once it was started, it increased to about 800 and then came down to about 500 when it was stopped.  He then had a recent bump again up to the 1000 and since Lipitor has been stopped, his CK apparently has returned to about the 500 or 600s.  I do no have any laboratory data to support these statements by the patient, but he seems to be up to speed on this.  More recently, he has been started taking Zetia. He does not have any proximal weakness.  He denies any myalgias.

naive.response_text:
I understand that you've been dealing with weakness and numbness in your right leg for quite some time, and I want to acknowledge how challenging that must be for you. It's important that we work together to figure out what's causing these symptoms and how we can address them effectively.

Based on what you've described, the weakness and numbness in your leg could be related to several potential issues, such as nerve compression or peripheral neuropathy. Given that you can't have an MRI due to your ear implant, we might consider other diagnostic tests, like a nerve conduction study or EMG, which you've already had. Your recent switch to Zetia doesn't seem to be causing issues, but we'll continue to monitor your CK levels to ensure they remain stable.

We'll move forward with the necessary steps to determine the cause of your symptoms and develop a treatment plan tailored to your needs. If needed, we may also explore other specialists or tests to gain more insight. Please feel free to ask any questions or share any concerns you have as we proceed. Your health and comfort are our top priorities.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all this information with me. It sounds like you've been dealing with these symptoms for a while, and I can understand how that might be concerning and frustrating. The weakness in your right leg and the ongoing tingling and numbness must be challenging, especially when it affects your daily activities. I want to make sure we explore this thoroughly together.

Given the duration of your symptoms and the history of elevated CK levels while on Lipitor, we should consider several possibilities. The weakness and numbness could be related to nerve issues, such as a pinched nerve in your spine, or it might be linked to muscle damage from the statins, though your CK levels have improved since stopping them. Since we couldn't do an MRI due to your ear implant, we might explore other imaging options or consider a follow-up EMG to assess nerve function [PMID ?].

We'll plan to monitor your CK levels closely and adjust your medications as needed. If your symptoms change or worsen, such as if you experience more severe weakness or new pain, it's important to reach out promptly. We'll also schedule a follow-up in 4-6 weeks to reassess your symptoms and treatment plan.

Before you leave, could you summarize what we discussed today? This will help ensure you understand the next steps and that I've addressed your concerns.

framework_only.response_text:
Hello! It's nice to meet you and hear about your concerns. You mentioned having weakness in your right leg for the past six months, which sounds like it's been a constant issue. I want to understand how this has been affecting your daily life.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly. Can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in the near future. In the meantime, if you notice any changes in your symptoms or if they get worse, please call us so we can address them promptly.
